examples.title: Algorithm: Raspberry Pi GPIO Control Flow
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the standard pattern for controlling GPIO pins on Raspberry Pi, including LED control and button reading
examples.code:
ALGORITHM GPIO_Control
INPUT: Pin numbers for LED and Button
OUTPUT: LED state changes based on button press

BEGIN
 # Initialize GPIO settings
 CALL SetGPIO_Mode(GPIO_BCM)
 
 # Configure LED pin as output
 CALL GPIO_Setup(led_pin, GPIO_OUT)
 
 # Configure Button pin as input with pull-down
 CALL GPIO_Setup(button_pin, GPIO_IN, pull_up_down=GPIO_PUD_DOWN)
 
 # Main control loop
 WHILE True DO
 # Read button state
 button_state = CALL GPIO_Input(button_pin)
 
 # Check if button is pressed
 IF button_state == HIGH THEN
 # Turn LED on
 CALL GPIO_Output(led_pin, HIGH)
 PRINT "Button pressed - LED ON"
 ELSE
 # Turn LED off
 CALL GPIO_Output(led_pin, LOW)
 PRINT "Button released - LED OFF"
 END IF
 
 # Small delay to debounce
 CALL Sleep(0.1)
 END WHILE
 
 # Cleanup GPIO pins on exit
 CALL GPIO_Cleanup()
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the fundamental GPIO control pattern on Raspberry Pi: (1) Set the GPIO mode to BCM numbering, (2) Configure pins as input or output, (3) Use a loop to continuously read inputs and control outputs, (4) Always call cleanup at the end to reset pins.

examples.title: Python: LED and Button Control with RPi.GPIO
examples.description: Complete Python implementation showing how to control an LED and read a button state using the RPi.GPIO library
examples.code:
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

# Define pin numbers (BCM numbering)
LED_PIN = 17 # Physical pin 11, BCM pin 17
BUTTON_PIN = 23 # Physical pin 16, BCM pin 23

def setup_gpio():
 """Initialize GPIO settings"""
 # Use BCM pin numbering (Broadcom chip pin numbers)
 GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
 
 # Configure LED pin as output (initially off)
 GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT, initial=GPIO.LOW)
 
 # Configure Button pin as input with pull-down resistor
 GPIO.setup(BUTTON_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_DOWN)

def control_led():
 """Main function to control LED based on button press"""
 try:
 print("Raspberry Pi GPIO Control Started")
 print(f"LED Pin: {LED_PIN}, Button Pin: {BUTTON_PIN}")
 print("Press Ctrl+C to exit\n")
 
 while True:
 # Read button state (HIGH when pressed, LOW when not)
 button_state = GPIO.input(BUTTON_PIN)
 
 if button_state == GPIO.HIGH:
 # Button pressed - turn LED on
 GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)
 print(f"Button Pressed -> LED ON")
 else:
 # Button released - turn LED off
 GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)
 print(f"Button Released -> LED OFF")
 
 # Debounce delay
 time.sleep(0.1)
 
 except KeyboardInterrupt:
 print("\nExiting program...")
 finally:
 # Cleanup GPIO pins to prevent warnings
 GPIO.cleanup()
 print("GPIO pins cleaned up. Goodbye!")

if __name__ == "__main__":
 setup_gpio()
 control_led()
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates the core GPIO control pattern: (1) Import RPi.GPIO library, (2) Set GPIO mode to BCM for pin numbering, (3) Setup LED pin as output and button as input with pull-down, (4) Main loop reads button state and controls LED accordingly, (5) Finally block ensures GPIO.cleanup() is called to reset pins. Time complexity is O(n) for the running loop, space complexity is O(1).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - linear time based on runtime

examples.title: Python: DHT11 Temperature and Humidity Sensor
examples.description: Implementation of a temperature monitoring system using DHT11 sensor with Adafruit_DHT library
examples.code:
import Adafruit_DHT
import time
import sys

# DHT sensor configuration
DHT_SENSOR = Adafruit_DHT.DHT11 # Sensor type
DHT_PIN = 17 # GPIO pin where sensor is connected

def read_sensor_data():
 """
 Read temperature and humidity from DHT11 sensor.
 Returns: tuple (humidity, temperature) or (None, None) on failure
 """
 # Attempt to read sensor data
 humidity, temperature = Adafruit_DHT.read(DHT_SENSOR, DHT_PIN)
 
 # Check if reading was successful
 if humidity is not None and temperature is not None:
 return humidity, temperature
 else:
 return None, None

def celsius_to_fahrenheit(celsius):
 """Convert Celsius to Fahrenheit"""
 return (celsius * 9/5) + 32

def display_readings(humidity, temperature, use_fahrenheit=False):
 """Display sensor readings in formatted output"""
 print("=" * 40)
 print(" TEMPERATURE MONITORING SYSTEM")
 print("=" * 40)
 
 if use_fahrenheit:
 temp_f = celsius_to_fahrenheit(temperature)
 print(f"Temperature: {temp_f:.1f}°F")
 else:
 print(f"Temperature: {temperature:.1f}°C")
 
 print(f"Humidity: {humidity:.1f}%")
 print("=" * 40)

def monitor_temperature(interval=2, duration=None, use_fahrenheit=False):
 """
 Continuously monitor temperature and humidity.
 
 Args:
 interval: Time between readings in seconds
 duration: Total monitoring time in seconds (None for infinite)
 use_fahrenheit: Display temperature in Fahrenheit if True
 """
 print("DHT11 Temperature & Humidity Monitor")
 print(f"Sensor Pin: GPIO{DHT_PIN}")
 print(f"Reading Interval: {interval} seconds")
 print("Press Ctrl+C to stop\n")
 
 start_time = time.time()
 
 try:
 while True:
 # Read sensor data
 humidity, temperature = read_sensor_data()
 
 if humidity is not None and temperature is not None:
 # Valid reading - display results
 display_readings(humidity, temperature, use_fahrenheit)
 else:
 # Sensor reading failed
 print("Failed to read sensor data. Retrying...")
 
 # Check if duration limit reached
 if duration and (time.time() - start_time) >= duration:
 print("\nMonitoring duration complete.")
 break
 
 # Wait for next reading
 time.sleep(interval)
 
 except KeyboardInterrupt:
 print("\nMonitoring stopped by user.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
 # Run with 2-second intervals, display in Celsius
 monitor_temperature(interval=2)
examples.explanation: This implementation shows temperature monitoring with DHT11 sensor: (1) Uses Adafruit_DHT library for sensor communication, (2) read() function returns humidity and temperature, (3) Includes error handling for failed readings, (4) Supports both Celsius and Fahrenheit output, (5) Configurable interval and monitoring duration. Requires: pip install Adafruit_DHT
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - linear time based on number of readings

examples.title: Python: LED Blink Patterns with gpiozero
examples.description: Demonstrating high-level GPIO control using the gpiozero library for cleaner LED patterns
examples.code:
from gpiozero import LED, Button, Buzzer
from gpiozero.pulses import PWMLED
from signal import pause
import time

# Initialize components using gpiozero (simplified API)
led_red = LED(17) # Red LED on GPIO 17
led_green = LED(27) # Green LED on GPIO 27
button = Button(23, pull_up=True) # Button on GPIO 23
buzzer = Buzzer(22) # Buzzer on GPIO 22

def blink_pattern(led, times=5, on_time=0.5, off_time=0.5):
 """
 Blink an LED a specified number of times.
 
 Args:
 led: LED object to control
 times: Number of blinks
 on_time: Duration LED stays on (seconds)
 off_time: Duration LED stays off (seconds)
 """
 for _ in range(times):
 led.on()
 time.sleep(on_time)
 led.off()
 time.sleep(off_time)

def sos_morse_code():
 """Send SOS signal: 3 short, 3 long, 3 short"""
 short = 0.2
 long = 0.6
 gap = 0.2
 
 # 3 short (S)
 for _ in range(3):
 led_red.on()
 time.sleep(short)
 led_red.off()
 time.sleep(gap)
 
 time.sleep(0.4) # Gap between letters
 
 # 3 long (O)
 for _ in range(3):
 led_red.on()
 time.sleep(long)
 led_red.off()
 time.sleep(gap)
 
 time.sleep(0.4) # Gap between letters
 
 # 3 short (S)
 for _ in range(3):
 led_red.on()
 time.sleep(short)
 led_red.off()
 time.sleep(gap)

def button_pressed():
 """Event handler for button press"""
 print("Button pressed!")
 led_green.on()
 buzzer.beep(0.1, 0.1, 3) # Beep 3 times
 led_green.off()

def button_released():
 """Event handler for button release"""
 print("Button released!")

def running_lights():
 """Create a running lights effect on multiple LEDs"""
 leds = [LED(17), LED(27), LED(22), LED(10)] # Multiple GPIO pins
 delay = 0.2
 
 while True:
 for i, led in enumerate(leds):
 led.on()
 time.sleep(delay)
 led.off()
 # Reverse direction
 for i in range(len(leds) - 2, 0, -1):
 leds[i].on()
 time.sleep(delay)
 leds[i].off()

def pwm_brightness_control():
 """Demonstrate PWM for variable brightness"""
 pwm_led = PWMLED(17) # Use PWM for brightness control
 
 # Gradually increase brightness
 print("Gradually increasing brightness...")
 for brightness in range(0, 101, 5):
 pwm_led.value = brightness / 100.0
 time.sleep(0.1)
 
 # Gradually decrease brightness
 print("Gradually decreasing brightness...")
 for brightness in range(100, -1, -5):
 pwm_led.value = brightness / 100.0
 time.sleep(0.1)

# Main execution - register event handlers
print("Raspberry Pi GPIO Control with gpiozero")
print("Press the button to trigger event handlers\n")

# Register button event handlers
button.when_pressed = button_pressed
button.when_released = button_released

# Run example demonstrations
print("Running SOS pattern...")
sos_morse_code()

print("\nDemonstrating PWM brightness control...")
pwm_brightness_control()

print("\nSetup complete - waiting for button presses...")
print("Press Ctrl+C to exit")

# Keep program running
pause()
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates the gpiozero library which provides a higher-level abstraction than RPi.GPIO: (1) LED class simplifies on/off control, (2) Button class with event handlers (when_pressed, when_released), (3) Buzzer for audio feedback, (4) PWMLED for variable brightness control, (5) Demonstrates SOS morse code pattern and running lights. gpiozero is more beginner-friendly than RPi.GPIO. Requires: pip install gpiozero
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - linear based on runtime and pattern iterations
